title: 10 Herbal Creams For Queasy Stomach
intro_desc: Herbal creams can provide relief for a queasy stomach, but another effective way to calm digestive issues is with herbal teas. These teas work by soothing the digestive system and reducing nausea, which can be caused by stress, motion sickness, or certain foods. The herbs in these teas have anti-inflammatory properties that help to ease stomach cramps and spasms, allowing the digestive system to function properly.  One example of an herb that can help with a queasy stomach is Zingiber officinale, also known as ginger. Ginger has natural anti-inflammatory properties that can help to reduce nausea and vomiting. It also has antibacterial properties that can help to prevent infections in the digestive system. Foeniculum vulgare, or fennel, is another herb that can help to calm digestive issues. It has a soothing effect on the stomach and can help to reduce gas and bloating.  Glycyrrhiza glabra, or licorice root, is also a useful herb for calming a queasy stomach. It has anti-inflammatory properties that can help to reduce stomach cramps and spasms, and it also has antibacterial properties that can help to prevent infections. When you drink herbal teas containing these herbs, you may notice that your queasy stomach starts to feel better within a short time. Your digestive system will be able to function properly again, and you'll be able to enjoy your meals without feeling uncomfortable or nauseous. Drinking herbal teas can bring many benefits to your life, including improved digestion, reduced nausea and vomiting, and a general feeling of well-being.

remedies.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale creams contains active constituents like gingerol and shogaol. These compounds have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, which help to ease stomach discomfort and nausea. The oils in ginger can also help to reduce inflammation in the digestive tract and improve digestion. The antispasmodic properties of ginger can help to calm spasms and cramping in the stomach, providing relief from queasiness and stomach upset. By reducing inflammation and soothing the stomach, Zingiber officinale creams can help to alleviate queasy stomach symptoms.
remedies.image_alt: Zingiber officinale herbal creams for queasy stomach
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 2 cups of coconut oil, 1 cup of shea butter, 1/2 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of ginger essential oil, and 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil.
Melt coconut oil, shea butter, and beeswax in a double boiler over low heat.
Remove from heat and let cool until mixture reaches 120°F to 130°F.
Add ginger essential oil and vitamin E oil to the mixture and stir well.
Pour mixture into a container and let it cool and harden before use.

remedies.remedy_desc: Foeniculum vulgare creams contains compounds like anethole, limonene, and pinene, which have been found in studies to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. These properties can help reduce inflammation and protect the stomach lining, making it a potential remedy for queasy stomach. Anethole, in particular, has been shown to have a calming effect on the digestive system, which can help alleviate nausea and discomfort. The antioxidants in Foeniculum vulgare creams can also help neutralize free radicals that can cause stomach upset. By reducing inflammation and protecting the stomach lining, Foeniculum vulgare creams may help alleviate queasy stomach symptoms.
remedies.image_alt: Foeniculum vulgare herbal creams for queasy stomach
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of Foeniculum vulgare seeds, 1/2 cup of coconut oil, 1/4 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil.
Dry the Foeniculum vulgare seeds in a low-temperature oven (150°F) for 1 hour. Let them cool.
Melt the coconut oil and beeswax in a double boiler. Stir until the beeswax dissolves.
Add the dried Foeniculum vulgare seeds and vitamin E oil to the melted mixture. Stir well.
Pour the mixture into small containers. Let it cool and solidify before use. Store in a cool, dry place.

remedies.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra creams contains glycyrrhizin and saponins, which are its active constituents. Glycyrrhizin helps to reduce inflammation in the digestive system, soothing the stomach and reducing queasiness. Saponins, on the other hand, have anti-spasmodic properties that calm down stomach muscles and prevent them from contracting and causing discomfort. The anti-inflammatory and anti-spasmodic properties of glycyrrhizin and saponins in Glycyrrhiza glabra creams help to alleviate symptoms of queasy stomach, such as nausea and cramps. By reducing inflammation and calming down stomach muscles, Glycyrrhiza glabra creams can provide relief from queasy stomach.
remedies.image_alt: Glycyrrhiza glabra herbal creams for queasy stomach
remedy_procedure: Gather 1 cup of dried Glycyrrhiza glabra root, 1 cup of coconut oil, 1 cup of shea butter, and 1 tablespoon of beeswax.
Grind the dried Glycyrrhiza glabra root into a fine powder using a coffee grinder or mortar and pestle.
Melt the coconut oil and shea butter in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water.
Add the ground Glycyrrhiza glabra root powder and beeswax to the melted coconut oil and shea butter mixture. Stir until well combined.
Pour the mixture into a heat-proof container and let it cool and solidify. Once solid, store the cream in an airtight container in the fridge for up to 2 weeks.

remedies.remedy_desc: Rosmarinus officinalis creams contains rosmarinic acid, carnosic acid, and borneol as its active constituents. These compounds have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help soothe an upset stomach. Rosmararinic acid, in particular, has been shown to reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, which can help alleviate queasiness and discomfort. The antispasmodic properties of carnosic acid can also help relax the muscles in the stomach and reduce spasms, further easing stomach upset. Additionally, borneol's anxiolytic properties can help calm the nervous system, reducing anxiety and stress-related stomach issues.
remedies.image_alt: Rosmarinus officinalis herbal creams for queasy stomach
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of coconut oil, 1/4 cup of beeswax, 1 tablespoon of rosemary essential oil, 1 tablespoon of vitamin E oil.
Melt coconut oil and beeswax in a saucepan over low heat, stirring until beeswax dissolves.
Remove saucepan from heat and let mixture cool slightly. Add rosemary essential oil and vitamin E oil, stirring well.
Pour mixture into a clean container and let it cool completely. This may take about 30 minutes.
Once cooled, your Rosmarinus officinalis cream is ready to use. Store it in an airtight container in the fridge for up to 2 weeks.

remedies.remedy_desc: Mentha x piperita creams contains the bioactive constituents limonene, menthone, and menthol. These compounds have antispasmodic properties, which help to relax the muscles in the stomach and reduce cramping. Menthone also has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to soothe and calm the stomach lining, reducing irritation and discomfort. Menthol has a cooling effect, which can help to calm nausea and queasiness by stimulating the senses and promoting a sense of relaxation. By combining these properties, Mentha x piperita creams can provide relief from queasy stomach symptoms.
remedies.image_alt: Mentha x piperita herbal creams for queasy stomach
remedy_procedure: Gather 1 cup of water and 1/4 cup of dried mint leaves (Mentha x piperita).
Boil the water and add the dried mint leaves. Let it steep for 10 minutes.
Strain the mixture and let it cool. You should get about 1/2 cup of mint extract.
Mix 1/4 cup of coconut oil, 1/4 cup of beeswax, and 2 tablespoons of shea butter in a saucepan.
Add the mint extract to the saucepan and heat it until the mixture is melted and smooth. Pour into a container and let it cool.

remedies.remedy_desc: Ginkgo biloba creams contains flavonoids and terpenoids, which are two main bioactive constituents. Flavonoids, such as quercetin and kaempferol, have anti-inflammatory properties that may help reduce nausea and stomach discomfort. Terpenoids, like ginkgolides, have a stabilizing effect on the stomach lining, reducing inflammation and alleviating queasiness. The antioxidant properties of these compounds also help protect the stomach lining from damage and reduce the risk of stomach ulcers. By reducing inflammation and protecting the stomach lining, Ginkgo biloba creams can help alleviate queasy stomach symptoms.
remedies.image_alt: Ginkgo biloba herbal creams for queasy stomach
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of coconut oil, 1/4 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of shea butter, 2 tablespoons of Ginkgo biloba extract, 2 teaspoons of vitamin E oil.
Melt coconut oil and beeswax in a double boiler or a microwave-safe bowl in 10-second increments, stirring between each heating.
Add shea butter and stir until melted. Remove from heat and let cool slightly.
Add Ginkgo biloba extract and vitamin E oil. Stir well to combine. Pour mixture into a mixing bowl and let cool until solid.
Use a spatula to shape the mixture into a cream. Store in an airtight container in the fridge for up to 2 weeks.

remedies.remedy_desc: Lavandula angustifolia creams contains linalool and linalyl acetate, two main bioactive constituents with anti-inflammatory properties. These compounds help to reduce inflammation in the stomach, which can cause queasiness. The antispasmodic properties of linalool and linalyl acetate also soothe stomach muscles, making them less likely to contract and cause discomfort. Additionally, the calming effects of Lavandula angustifolia can help to reduce anxiety and stress, both of which can exacerbate queasiness. The anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties of Lavandula angustifolia creams make it a potential remedy for soothing a queasy stomach.
remedies.image_alt: Lavandula angustifolia herbal creams for queasy stomach
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of coconut oil, 1/2 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of lavender essential oil, and 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil.
Melt coconut oil and beeswax in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water.
Remove melted mixture from heat and stir in lavender essential oil and vitamin E oil.
Let mixture cool and thicken. This may take about 30 minutes, depending on temperature.
Pour mixture into small containers and let cool completely before use. Store in a cool, dark place.

remedies.remedy_desc: Curcuma longa creams contains a compound called curcumin, which is responsible for its therapeutic properties. Curcumin has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which help to soothe an upset stomach. The curcumin in Curcuma longa creams also has a compound called turmerone, which has been shown to have a protective effect on the digestive system. Additionally, the cream contains another compound called demethoxycurcumin, which has been found to have a calming effect on the stomach muscles. By reducing inflammation and promoting digestive health, Curcuma longa creams can help alleviate queasiness and discomfort associated with an upset stomach.
remedies.image_alt: Curcuma longa herbal creams for queasy stomach
remedy_procedure: Melt 1/2 cup of coconut oil in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl over a pot of boiling water.
Add 2 tablespoons of turmeric powder (Curcuma longa) to the melted coconut oil. Stir well.
Add 1/4 cup of beeswax to the mixture and stir until melted.
Remove from heat and add 2 tablespoons of shea butter and 2 tablespoons of olive oil. Stir until combined.
Pour the mixture into a jar or tin and let it cool and harden before use. Store in a cool place.

remedies.remedy_desc: Melissa officinalis creams contains active constituents like linalool, linalyl acetate, and geraniol, which have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties. These compounds help to calm the digestive system and reduce inflammation in the stomach. Melissa officinalis creams also contain rosmarinic acid, which has antioxidant properties that can help to protect the stomach lining from damage. The combination of these properties can help to soothe a queasy stomach and reduce symptoms of indigestion. By reducing inflammation and promoting relaxation, Melissa officinalis creams can provide relief from stomach discomfort.
remedies.image_alt: Melissa officinalis herbal creams for queasy stomach
remedy_procedure: Gather 1 cup of dried Melissa officinalis leaves and 1 cup of carrier oil (coconut or olive oil) in a clean bowl.
Steep the Melissa leaves in the carrier oil for 2-3 weeks in a cool dark place, shaking the bowl every day.
Strain the oil through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another bowl, discarding the leaves.
Mix 2 tablespoons of the infused oil with 2 tablespoons of beeswax in a small saucepan over low heat, stirring until the beeswax melts.
Remove from heat and let cool. Pour the mixture into small containers and let it harden before use.

remedies.remedy_desc: Cinnamomum verum creams contains oils rich in cinnamaldehyde, a key bioactive constituent that helps to reduce nausea and alleviate queasy stomachs. Cinnamaldehyde has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to soothe the stomach lining and reduce irritation. The oils in Cinnamomum verum creams also contain eugenol, a natural analgesic that can help to ease stomach discomfort. Additionally, the creams contain linalool, a compound that has a calming effect on the digestive system, helping to regulate digestion and reduce queasiness. The combination of these bioactive constituents in Cinnamomum verum creams may help to provide relief from a queasy stomach.
remedies.image_alt: Cinnamomum verum herbal creams for queasy stomach
remedy_procedure: Gather 2 cups of coconut oil, 1 cup of shea butter, and 1/4 cup of beeswax in a double boiler.
Melt the coconut oil, shea butter, and beeswax in the double boiler over low heat, stirring occasionally.
Add 2 tablespoons of Cinnamomum verum essential oil to the melted mixture and stir well.
Remove the mixture from heat and let it cool until it reaches 120°F to 130°F.
Pour the mixture into small containers and let it cool completely before use. Store in a cool place.

faq_prevent: Drinking herbal tea may help with a queasy stomach. Some teas, like peppermint and ginger, have properties that can calm the digestive system and reduce nausea. These teas are often consumed when people feel queasy, and many find them helpful in alleviating stomach discomfort.
faq_safe: Herbal teas can be a good option for a queasy stomach, but it's best to use them in moderation. Drinking a cup of herbal tea every day is unlikely to cause harm, but it's also not guaranteed to help. Some herbal teas may interact with medications or worsen underlying health issues, so it's essential to monitor your body's response.
faq_timeline: Herbal teas can help calm a queasy stomach. Some people notice a difference within 30 minutes to an hour after drinking. Ginger tea, in particular, is known to ease nausea quickly. If you're feeling queasy, try sipping on a warm cup of herbal tea to see if it helps settle your stomach.
faq_time: If you have a queasy stomach, try drinking herbal tea in the morning. A gentle cup before breakfast can help calm your stomach and set you up for the day. Many herbal teas, like peppermint or ginger, can ease nausea and make you feel more comfortable. Give it a try and see how it works for you.
faq_interaction: Herbal teas can interact with prescription medications for a queasy stomach. Some herbs, like peppermint and ginger, can make stomach problems worse if mixed with certain medications. For example, taking peppermint with antacids can decrease their effectiveness. This means you should be careful when drinking herbal teas with prescription medication.
faq_pregnant: When you're pregnant and feeling queasy, herbal teas can be a soothing option. Ginger and peppermint teas are often recommended because they can ease nausea. However, it's essential to choose teas that are safe during pregnancy, as some herbs can be too strong or even cause complications. Always check the ingredients and look for reputable brands.
faq_breastfeeding: Herbal teas can be a good option for a queasy stomach while breastfeeding. However, be cautious with strong herbs like peppermint and ginger, as they can stimulate breast milk production. Mild teas like chamomile and fennel may be safer to try. Some herbs can affect the taste of breast milk, so watch for any changes.
title_2: Queasy Stomach and Pregnancy: 10 Herbal Creams for Comfort
